title: A teacher's reply
summary: A parent has written to ask that her son's B+ be raised to an A 'for his university application'. The literature teacher replies — politely, firmly, and with an offer that has nothing to do with this grade.
body:
Dear Mrs Le,

Thank you for your message about Khang's essay grade. I have read it carefully, and I have re-read the essay. I would like to take a moment to explain, with as much honesty as I can, why the grade will remain a B+.

First, the request itself. You have written that Khang 'worked very hard' on this essay, and I believe you. I saw the early drafts. I saw the rewriting. Effort, however, is not what an essay grade is principally measuring. It is measuring how clearly the essay communicates an argument to a reader who is not Khang's parent. On that specific test, this essay sits, in my judgement, in the B+ range — strong, but with two paragraphs whose claims I had to read twice to follow.

I want to address a worry I sense behind your message. You have mentioned, more than once, that the difference between a B+ and an A could matter for university application. I understand this concern. I cannot, in good conscience, adjust a grade for that reason. If I did, I would be telling Khang, quietly, that grades reflect parental advocacy rather than the work on the page. He is too clever not to notice that lesson, and it would be the wrong one to learn at sixteen.

I would also be unfair to the students whose parents did not write to me. Some of them produced essays in the same B+ band, and they did not receive the upgrade you are asking me to give Khang. To raise his grade now would be to set their grades, by silence, lower than his. I am not willing to do that.

There is a more useful conversation we could have, and I would welcome it. I have written specific comments in the margins of Khang's essay about the two unclear paragraphs. He could revise these for me — not for re-grading on this piece, which is closed, but as practice for the next assignment. If he wants, I am happy to read his next draft early and give him notes before submission. That is the kind of help I can offer, and it costs me nothing.

I want to end with this. You are clearly an attentive parent, and I see the result of that in Khang's classroom presence. He is curious, he is kind to slower classmates, and he writes with growing confidence. None of these are small things. They will outlast every grade I give him.

If you would still like to meet, I am free on Thursday after school. Please bring Khang. The conversation will be more useful with him in the room.

With respect,
Mr Phong
Literature, Year 11

annotations.meaningVi: sự bênh vực, sự can thiệp ủng hộ
sự nâng cấp; ở đây: việc nâng điểm
tồn tại lâu hơn, sống dài hơn

grammarNotes.title: Polite firmness: 'I cannot, in good conscience…' and 'I am not willing to do that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn phong trang trọng, thay vì nói thẳng 'No' hay 'I won't', người viết thường dùng các cách diễn đạt mềm hơn nhưng vẫn kiên quyết. Trong bài: 'I cannot, in good conscience, adjust a grade for that reason.' (Tôi không thể, vì lương tâm, điều chỉnh điểm vì lý do đó.) Và sau đó: 'I am not willing to do that.' Hai cách này từ chối nhưng giữ tôn trọng — và đặt sự từ chối trên nền tảng đạo đức / nguyên tắc, không phải sở thích cá nhân.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In formal writing, instead of a flat 'No' or 'I won't', writers often use softer but firmer phrasings. From the letter: 'I cannot, in good conscience, adjust a grade for that reason.' And later: 'I am not willing to do that.' Both refuse politely, and both ground the refusal in principle rather than personal preference.

cloze.template:
Dear Mrs Le,

Thank you for your message about Khang's essay grade. I have read it carefully, and I have re-read the essay. I would like to take a moment to explain, with as much {{b1}} as I can, why the grade will remain a B+.

First, the request itself. You have written that Khang 'worked very hard' on this essay, and I believe you. I saw the early drafts. I saw the rewriting. {{b2}}, however, is not what an essay grade is principally measuring. It is measuring how clearly the essay {{b3}} an argument to a reader who is not Khang's parent. On that specific test, this essay sits, in my {{b4}}, in the B+ range — strong, but with two paragraphs whose claims I had to read twice to follow.

I want to address a worry I sense behind your message. You have mentioned, more than once, that the difference between a B+ and an A could matter for university {{b5}}. I understand this concern. I cannot, in good conscience, adjust a grade for that reason. If I did, I would be telling Khang, quietly, that grades reflect parental {{b6}} rather than the work on the page. He is too clever not to notice that lesson, and it would be the wrong one to learn at {{b7}}.

I would also be {{b8}} to the students whose parents did not write to me. Some of them produced essays in the same B+ band, and they did not receive the {{b9}} you are asking me to give Khang. To raise his grade now would be to set their grades, by silence, lower than his. I am not willing to do that.

There is a more useful conversation we could have, and I would welcome it. I have written specific comments in the {{b10}} of Khang's essay about the two unclear paragraphs. He could {{b11}} these for me — not for re-grading on this piece, which is closed, but as practice for the next assignment. If he wants, I am happy to read his next {{b12}} early and give him notes before submission. That is the kind of help I can offer, and it costs me nothing.

I want to end with this. You are clearly an {{b13}} parent, and I see the result of that in Khang's classroom presence. He is curious, he is kind to slower classmates, and he writes with growing confidence. None of these are small things. They will {{b14}} every grade I give him.

If you would still like to meet, I am free on {{b15}} after school. Please bring Khang. The conversation will be more useful with him in the {{b16}}.

With respect,
Mr Phong
Literature, Year 11

blanks.explanation: He will explain 'with as much honesty as I can' — setting the tone of the letter.
Effort is not what an essay grade is principally measuring — the central point Mr Phong has to make.
It measures how clearly the essay communicates an argument to a reader who is not the writer's parent.
'In my judgement' — the teacher's professional opinion.
The difference between B+ and A could matter for university application — the parent's worry.
Grades would reflect parental advocacy — parents' active lobbying — rather than the work on the page.
It would be the wrong lesson to learn at sixteen — Khang's age.
He would be unfair to the students whose parents did not write to him — the principle of equal treatment.
They did not receive the upgrade you are asking me to give Khang — silently raising his grade would lower theirs by comparison.
Comments in the margins of the essay — the white space at the edges of the page.
Khang could revise these paragraphs — improve them — not for a new grade, but for practice.
Mr Phong offers to read Khang's next draft early — before he submits the final version.
Mrs Le is an attentive parent — one who pays close attention to her child — and Mr Phong sees the good result of that in class.
Khang's qualities (curiosity, kindness, growing confidence) will outlast every grade Mr Phong gives him.
Mr Phong is free on Thursday after school for a meeting if Mrs Le still wants one.
The conversation will be more useful with Khang in the room — Mr Phong insists the student is present.

translationVi:
Kính gửi cô Lê,

Cảm ơn cô đã gửi tin nhắn về điểm bài luận của Khang. Tôi đã đọc kỹ tin nhắn, và đã đọc lại bài luận. Tôi xin phép dành một chút thời gian để giải thích, với mức độ thành thật nhất có thể, vì sao điểm sẽ vẫn là B+.

Đầu tiên là về chính lời đề nghị. Cô đã viết rằng Khang 'đã làm rất chăm' trong bài luận này, và tôi tin cô. Tôi đã thấy những bản nháp đầu. Tôi đã thấy quá trình viết lại. Tuy nhiên, sự nỗ lực không phải là cái mà điểm bài luận chủ yếu đo lường. Nó đo lường mức độ bài luận truyền đạt một lập luận rõ ràng đến một người đọc không phải là cha mẹ của Khang. Trên bài kiểm tra cụ thể đó, theo đánh giá của tôi, bài luận này nằm ở khoảng B+ — mạnh, nhưng có hai đoạn mà tôi phải đọc lại hai lần mới theo được lập luận.

Tôi muốn nói đến một lo lắng mà tôi cảm nhận được phía sau lời của cô. Cô đã đề cập, hơn một lần, rằng khác biệt giữa B+ và A có thể quan trọng cho hồ sơ vào đại học. Tôi hiểu mối lo này. Vì lương tâm, tôi không thể điều chỉnh điểm vì lý do đó. Nếu tôi làm vậy, tôi sẽ ngầm nói với Khang rằng điểm số phản ánh sự can thiệp của cha mẹ chứ không phải bài làm trên trang giấy. Em quá thông minh để không nhận ra bài học đó, và đó là bài học sai để học khi mười sáu tuổi.

Tôi cũng sẽ không công bằng với các học sinh khác mà cha mẹ không viết thư cho tôi. Một số em làm bài luận cùng nhóm B+, và họ không nhận được sự nâng điểm mà cô đang đề nghị tôi dành cho Khang. Nâng điểm cho Khang lúc này sẽ là, trong im lặng, đặt điểm của các em đó thấp hơn em. Tôi không sẵn lòng làm vậy.

Có một cuộc trò chuyện có ích hơn mà chúng ta có thể có, và tôi rất hoan nghênh. Tôi đã viết những nhận xét cụ thể ở lề bài luận của Khang về hai đoạn chưa rõ. Em có thể sửa lại các đoạn đó cho tôi — không phải để được chấm lại bài này (bài này đã xong), mà để luyện tập cho bài kế tiếp. Nếu em muốn, tôi sẵn lòng đọc bản nháp tiếp theo của em sớm và cho em nhận xét trước khi nộp. Đó là kiểu giúp đỡ tôi có thể đưa ra, và nó không tốn gì của tôi.

Tôi muốn kết bằng điều này. Cô rõ ràng là một phụ huynh quan tâm, và tôi thấy kết quả của điều đó trong sự có mặt của Khang trên lớp. Em ham hiểu biết, em tử tế với các bạn học chậm hơn, và em viết với sự tự tin đang lớn dần. Đó không phải là những điều nhỏ. Chúng sẽ sống lâu hơn mọi điểm số tôi cho em.

Nếu cô vẫn muốn gặp, tôi rảnh chiều thứ Năm sau giờ học. Mong cô đưa Khang đến cùng. Cuộc trò chuyện sẽ hữu ích hơn nếu em có mặt trong phòng.

Trân trọng,
Thầy Phong
Văn học, lớp 11

questions.prompt: Why has Mrs Le written to Mr Phong?
options: To ask that Khang's B+ on an essay be raised to an A
To enrol Khang in a new class
questions.explanation: The whole letter is a reply to a request to adjust the grade; the difference between B+ and A is at issue throughout.
questions.hint: Look at the first two paragraphs.

questions.prompt: Does Mr Phong dispute that Khang worked hard?
options: No — he says he believes her and saw the drafts and the rewriting
questions.explanation: 'You have written that Khang "worked very hard" on this essay, and I believe you. I saw the early drafts. I saw the rewriting.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Phong say an essay grade is principally measuring?
options: How clearly the essay communicates an argument to a reader who is not the writer's parent
questions.explanation: 'It is measuring how clearly the essay communicates an argument to a reader who is not Khang's parent.'

questions.prompt: What specific weakness does Mr Phong identify in the essay?
options: Two paragraphs whose claims he had to read twice to follow
questions.explanation: 'With two paragraphs whose claims I had to read twice to follow.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: What worry does Mr Phong sense behind Mrs Le's message?
options: That Khang will leave the school
That the B+/A difference could matter for Khang's university application
That Mrs Le is angry with him personally
That Khang is unhappy at home
questions.explanation: 'You have mentioned, more than once, that the difference between a B+ and an A could matter for university application.'

questions.prompt: Why won't Mr Phong adjust the grade for the university-application reason?
options: Because the school does not allow changes
Because doing so would teach Khang that grades reflect parental advocacy rather than the work on the page
questions.explanation: 'If I did, I would be telling Khang, quietly, that grades reflect parental advocacy rather than the work on the page.'

questions.prompt: What second reason does Mr Phong give for refusing?
options: He would be unfair to students whose parents did not write to him and whose own B+ essays did not receive an upgrade
Mrs Le did not pay him enough
questions.explanation: 'I would also be unfair to the students whose parents did not write to me.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Phong mean by 'set their grades, by silence, lower than his'?
options: He would announce their grades publicly
Raising Khang's grade without raising theirs would effectively demote them, even without saying so
Their parents would complain to the school
questions.explanation: An unannounced upgrade for one student silently changes the relative position of others.
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 4 — what does raising one grade do to others in the same band?

questions.prompt: What 'more useful conversation' does Mr Phong propose?
options: Khang can revise the two unclear paragraphs — not for re-grading on this piece (which is closed) but as practice; Mr Phong is also willing to read his next draft early
Move Khang to another teacher's class
questions.explanation: 'He could revise these for me — not for re-grading on this piece, which is closed, but as practice for the next assignment.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Phong say about Khang's qualities in class?
options: He is curious, kind to slower classmates, and writes with growing confidence — qualities that will outlast every grade
He is the best student in the class
questions.explanation: 'He is curious, he is kind to slower classmates, and he writes with growing confidence. None of these are small things. They will outlast every grade I give him.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Phong ask Mrs Le to bring if she meets him on Thursday?
options: Khang himself — the conversation will be more useful with him in the room
questions.explanation: 'Please bring Khang. The conversation will be more useful with him in the room.'
questions.hint: Look at the last short paragraph before the sign-off.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the letter?
options: Polite, firm, and grounded in principle — Mr Phong validates the parent's effort and the boy's qualities but does not move on the grade
questions.explanation: He acknowledges effort, explains his reasons, offers a constructive alternative, and ends warmly — without giving in.
questions.hint: Notice how he refuses and what he offers.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to Mr Phong's central principle?
options: Grades should reflect the work on the page, judged by the same standard for every student.
Grades are arbitrary and do not matter.
questions.explanation: Both his reasons — what an essay grade measures, and fairness to other students — point to the same principle.
questions.hint: Look at the two reasons in paragraphs 3 and 4.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Mr Phong NOT claiming?
options: That Khang's essay was strong overall
That Khang did not work hard
That fairness to other students matters
questions.explanation: 'You have written that Khang "worked very hard" on this essay, and I believe you.' He affirms the effort.

questions.prompt: What is Mr Phong offering Mrs Le instead of a grade change?
options: Concrete margin comments, optional revision practice, early feedback on the next draft, and a meeting that includes Khang
Permission to skip the next essay
questions.explanation: Paragraphs 5 and 7 list all of these — a constructive alternative the parent did not ask for.
questions.hint: Look at paragraphs 5 and the meeting offer at the end.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Phong refuses to change the grade in part because doing so would 'tell Khang, quietly, that grades reflect parental advocacy rather than the work on the page.' Think of a time when an adult in your life held a line that benefited you in the long run but felt unhelpful in the moment. What lesson did the refusal itself teach — beyond the original issue?
